title: English muffins
subtitle: Yeasted griddle muffins with a soft crumb and a nook-filled interior
excerpt: Yeasted dough rings cooked in a dry pan over a low heat, then split with a fork to reveal an open, nook-filled crumb. The fork-split matters: a knife would compress the texture that makes them worth making. Makes twelve.
sourceNotes: English muffins are documented in British household baking records from the eighteenth century. They are distinct from American muffins (which are quick-baked, cake-like items) and from the crumpet. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: muffins are documented as a griddle-cooked yeasted bread dusted with semolina or flour before cooking, sold by street muffin men and also made at home. The semolina dusting and the griddle cooking method are consistent across nineteenth-century British household baking records.

content.text: English muffins need to be cooked low and slow. The pan must be hot enough to colour the base but not so hot that it burns before the interior is cooked through. Test the heat with a single muffin first: the base should take about 6 minutes to turn deep gold. If it colours in 3, the pan is too hot. Always
them; a knife will flatten the crumb.
Make the dough and bulk ferment, 75 minutes

Combine the {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{yeast-dried}} in a large bowl. Add the warm {{whole-milk}}, 140 ml warm water, and melted {{unsalted-butter}}. Mix to a soft, slightly sticky dough. Knead for 8 to 10 minutes until smooth and elastic. Cover and leave for 75 minutes until doubled.
Shape and cook, 12 to 14 minutes per batch

Scatter {{semolina}} generously over a clean surface. Roll the dough to 1.5 cm thick and cut into rounds using a 9 cm cutter. Place on a semolina-dusted tray. Dust the tops with more semolina. Leave for 30 minutes.

Heat a dry frying pan or griddle over medium-low heat. Cook the muffins four at a time for 6 to 7 minutes per side, until deep gold on each face and the sides no longer look raw and doughy. Pressing the side should feel firm, not soft. Cool on a rack. To serve, insert a fork around the edge and pull the two halves apart.
